He made a promise to Her Highness. Is this what he calls 'protection' for Babel? All I see are crackdowns on Babel and mercenaries running wild!
There are some things that should not be said aloud. Especially not by you, Ascalon.
If you don't like it, arrest me.
Let's see if you've improved at all under his tutelage.
The General has taught me that there are more important things than violence.
And by the looks of things, the mercenaries aren't the only ruthless cutthroats around here.
Stop dodging if you dare! I'll prove to Their Highnesses that I can beat you!
Too slow, Manfred.

Wai—
Argh!
You're dead. Again.
Ugh...
Get up.
Don't... need you... to tell... me...
Footwork, Manfred. And Ascalon... stop going for his head all the time?
This is the most effective way. It's his own fault for getting hit.
If he can't dodge it, he has to learn to take it.
*sigh*...
You're right, Ascalon. But why did you stop? We didn't tell you to.
I... I can take it, Your Highness. It's not over yet!
Hmph.
Huh? I hit... Your Highness! I hit—
Did you, now?
Let me down, Ascalon!
Don't hurt him, Ascalon.
Manfred's long limbs flail helplessly in the air, while his torso is tied to a wooden stake.
H-How did you do it so fast?!
Got my inspiration from the way Her Highness sews.
Manfred wriggles to break free, but the more he squirms, the tighter the ropes hug him.
He cannot understand how Ascalon can be so strong and fast, when she looks as thin and frail as him.
Let—me—down!
Look, I won again!
Ascalon raises her weapon high and grins victoriously at the twin sovereigns.
Ascalon has gotten a lot more cheerful.
What are you thinking?
They're different from the other Sarkaz children.
We can change them, but how can we reach all the children of Kazdel?
Give me time. Babel can do it.
Time is a luxury.
I know. But education is not something that can be rushed. What we're doing is leaving hope for the next generation.
The flame of hope will never go out, as long as a single teacher in Kazdel remains willing to share with these children the vision of a beautiful future.
Theresa casts her eyes towards the two children, full of hope for the future.
Well? Are you going to admit defeat or not?
No! L-Let me down! We're not done!
I'll let you down when you admit defeat.
No way!
I'll unbind you, Manfred.
Thank you, Your Highness... W-woah, why am I flying?! Y-Your Highness!
Hehe.
...Hehe.
Alright, that will do, Theresa. Let him down.
You've improved. But Theresis hasn't taught you everything he knows.
You were always the faster learner, but ever since you left the general, you've been nothing but unadjusted and lost. He is disappointed in your departure, by the way.
I may not be as good with words as you are, but at least I don't just ignore the insults against Her Highness Theresa.
Do you really know why Her Highness is so dedicated to Babel's ideals? Do you really know why the General hasn't openly denounced the attacks on Babel?
You should have talked to him, rather than turning your back on him.
I turned my back? You're the ones who turned your backs on Her Highness and the voices of so many.
It's Theresis who has turned a blind eye to the mercenaries' acts of violence and the damage they've caused Babel! This much I know.
The General has allowed it precisely because he can't ignore the voices of the majority.
If the fate of the Sarkaz is doomed to fall into the abyss, what's wrong with using more effective means to prevent it?
So that's what you truly think.
It's what most of us in Kazdel think.
It's not that I don't trust Her Highness... anyone can promise a better future. But we can't ignore the reality before it.
Isn't it crueler to use violence to force a child to shake hands and make peace with the murderer who just killed his father?
Say whatever you want.
I'll keep my hands off the Military Commission. But if you try to act through the mercenaries...
Then my job is simple.
No more mercenaries, no more problems.
Ascalon!
...If you have the time.
Go visit Her Highness. I've never seen her so tired.
A puff of mist is the only sign that she was ever there.
Manfred looks down and wipes away the blood slowly seeping out of his chest.
He sighs and turns into the empty street, only to spot the deceased mercenary's effects.
Someday... we will all understand.
It was never Her Highness or the General who made the choice.
Who's there?
He hesitates before the door.
It has been a busy day at this old, ramshackle house, from the 'Teacher' to the multiple visitors.
Who could it be outside the door?
Has father finally returned? And if so, what should he say?
He has learned so much from the 'Teacher' that he wants to share with his father, whose visits back home have become less and less frequent.
He takes a deep breath, collects his mind, puts on a calm face, and opens the door.
Hello...?
Good evening.
You're... er, can I help you, sir?
You know who I am?
No sir, but I know the uniform of the Military Commission.
Just a routine inspection. Do you recognize these items?
This is... my father's—!
There have been some disturbances in the city, and I'm afraid your father... I'm so sorry.
Wh-Where did you find them? Was there...?
There was nothing else left at the scene. I'm sorry.
What's your name?
...Odda.
If you need any help...
No... no, I'm very grateful that you brought these back.
It's been a long time since I last saw him... this is enough.
I don't have to wait anymore... He's not coming back...
Do you have family? Or friends you can count on?
I can take care of myself. Besides, we see things like this every day in this city, no?
I-I'm sorry I gushed, sir. I just feel like I know you, somehow...
I'm sure you're busy. I won't keep you any longer.
My condolences.
The door closes. Manfred can hear the sound of sobbing coming from behind the door, but he doesn't feel sorrow. Not anymore.
Odda's right; he's seen this too many times. Yet he remembers this is not what the city was meant to be.
The nomadic city of Kazdel, Kazdel
Let me through!
This area is off-limits.
What happened?
Are you with Babel?
N-No.
Then it's none of your business.
The wall of Royal Court soldiers prevents Odda from passing.
But he sees several familiar, crying faces in the scattering crowd.
Those students? Hm... I think I can take this alley here...
Excuse me, do you know what happened?
Someone beat another guy to death. He's dead, too. Are you trying to join the racket?
Babel again. What is Her Highness thinking?
Shut your trap. You owe everything you eat, everything you wear, and the roof over your head to Her Highness.
Shh...
Well, it's simple enough alright! A teacher beat the father of his student to death!
A Teacher? How could...?
All the man did was scold him a little! And it was the teacher who was telling the kids all sorts of awful things!
That's not true...
You're a Babel shill too? Kids these days...
Hey, what are you doing?
Odda squeezes through the crowd and sees some familiar faces lying on the ground.
He finally finds the source of the commotion, the 'Teacher' lying in a cloud of dust.
Teacher! What's going on?
I... I didn't mean to hurt... his father...
Hang in there! I'll find a doctor!
No... over there... him...
Odda looks in the direction that the dying man is pointing, and sees an injured child crying by the lifeless body of his father.
Odda runs through the burning streets, carrying the injured child in his arms, and pieces together the whole picture from what he sees and hears.
It began when an agitated teacher accidentally killed an agitated father, before he was set upon by the angry crowd and fell in the dust of the streets.
The turmoil dragged everyone in: Civilians, Babel, mercenaries, even the Military Commission.
Like a chain reaction, clouds of dust were stirred up in various corners of the city.
It wasn't until artillery from an unknown source blasted through a wall of Babel's office building that the Royal Court troops finally quelled the riot.
It began with an accident and ended with a shell that caused more damage to Babel than anything in the past eighteen years, since the end of the war.
The kid is hurt and needs a doctor. Don't make me do this the hard way.
Whose side are you on? Babel? The Military Commission?
Neither! I just need to get him to a doctor. Now get out of my way!
My dad...
It's too late for him... I'm sorry.
Go. But don't trust outsiders so easily... Sarkaz. Not in this chaos.
Thank you...
Odda can feel the child's breathing grow weaker as he continues on his way.
Running through the streets, he seems to feel someone brush past his shoulder, but sees no one.
Was I imagining things?
Stop right there! Not one step closer. There are patients inside.
This kid needs medical attention, now!
But... oh... I didn't think there was anyone still willing to bring patients here.
Alright, I'll take it from here. I'll try to give you some extra medicine before we leave.
Leave?
Her Highness made the decision, but we all know what's happening. We're not welcome in Kazdel anymore.
Where are you going? Outside the city? Aren't you afraid of the dangers in the wastes?
...Yes, I am.
But if this city no longer welcomes us, then we respect its choice.
I want to join Babel. I can help care for the patients, or protect you.
Is this the one who started the whole mess? Will he live?
Don't think so. Lost too much blood.
Then what are we even doing here?
Just follow the General's orders and don't ask questions. Who'd have thought something so minor would need Their Highnesses' statement?
Ugh...
Is he trying to talk to us? There's nobody else around.
Probably just seeing things before he dies. No Banshee would sing an elegy for such a sinner.
It's... dangerous...
Go...
They can't see me. My Arts hide my presence.
Who... are... you...?
A student.
I came from far away, following in the footsteps of my mother, to find answers in this wandering city, and her two rulers.
Did you... find...?
I'm afraid the city doesn't have the answers ready yet.
But I found you. The ideas that you advocated were very interesting.
I was with you when you taught those children in that basement, learning about the city and Babel.
Of course, no one noticed... except Ascalon.
The... boy...
Has been brought to Babel where his injuries will be treated.
Do you regret your end?
No... the boy defended me...
He understood... what I tried to change...
I'm sorry... about his father...
I sympathize. It was not your intention to cause this tragedy, but fate has played a cruel joke on you.
I hear... crying...
It is your elegy, calling your fading soul to the other side.
Fear not, hesitate not. The myriad souls welcome you with open arms. Let my song take you beyond.
Thank you...
The young Banshee's song soothes the mind of the dying man.
The gentle song echoes through the crowded streets of the city. Banshees respond to the song of their young kin, adding their voices to the harmony.
It is an elegy that commemorates the passing of an ordinary Sarkaz.
'You were talking about the future of Babel in the lesson, but you didn't get to the end. What were you going to say?'
'I believe... that Babel will die someday. I hope I'm wrong.'
A song. Another Sarkaz has passed on in the turmoil.
There is no longer any place for Babel here. I will lead them and leave this city.
Theresa... we love you, all of us...
I know. But Babel has no choice.
But if you leave...
My people have made their choice. Neither I nor Theresis can change it.
We cannot abandon hope. But the best course of action, right now, is to avoid conflict.
...Hatred will swallow us like a flood.
You've always known... Theresa, our hero, our King.
Gentle requests and gradual changes will not sway the minds of the Sarkaz drawn here by Kazdel's rise.
Laqeramaline, I...
Perhaps what I say will disappoint, even hurt you, Theresa.
Rim Billiton, Sargon, even Columbia... you have opened their doors, and allowed the Sarkaz to negotiate with some as equals.
Terra has seen more technological progress in fifty years than it has for millennia.
...But we were too slow. Despite the incredible changes you've brought to Kazdel, they haven't been enough to bear the fruits of true transformation.
Even if I, too, believe that the fruits of spring are near.
Is this the stance of the Banshee Court, Laqeramaline?
I'm just a mother who came to see her boy off, Theresa.
I respect his choice, and there will come a day when he will speak for our Royal Court.
But that day is not today.
I know you love him... you haven't allowed yourself to age since he was born.
Aefanyl was a miracle to me, and to all Banshees.
The day he was born, I became loath to age with time.
I have preserved my appearance at the peak of my beauty... I wish to remain eternal in his memory.
My life is finite, of course. It's only the facade that never changes.
I know what Your Highness is thinking.
You never expected your dream to come true in this generation. You see yourself as the soil, and it's not the soil's due to witness the flowers bloom.
Even if the two of you have to give everything to nourish this soil...
Humor my little self-indulgence, Your Highness.
Until he finds the answer he is seeking, allow him to travel between the two of you, to learn from both of you.
He will be the first sprout to emerge from the soil.
Bring Aefanyl. Take care of him. Protect him in my place.
I will, Laqeramaline, my friend.
Thank you.
No matter where you go, no matter what fate awaits either of you, the Banshee Court will sing both your elegies, and announce to all...
This is my promise. It is the most ancient and powerful of witchcrafts, my final parting gift to you.
Laqeramaline.
Will we meet again?
I hope so.
Until next time, then. I look forward to the day that I can hear you sing in the mists of the Convallis.
Farewell, Your Highness. I'm afraid I must stay here and go no further.
Aefanyl will be part of your procession, after all... and I'm not ready to say goodbye to him yet.
Two days later
How long do you plan on standing there?
You came a little too early, if it's Theresa you're looking for.
I didn't think you would have the courage to come to me, Ascalon.
Why?
Do you mean why wouldn't you have the courage to see me, or why I banished Babel?
You've seen Manfred?
Yes.
Did you do it?
Not all the way.
Good.
Now, remove yourself before you say something childish and naive. Not even Theresa would agree with you.
Or you could choose to stay. You don't have to explain yourself. Afterall, you never formally joined Babel.
Her Highness needs someone to protect her.
She is more powerful than you think.
And I agree with you even less, Master.
I suppose I should take that as a no.
You and Manfred are my best students, yet both of you have your fatal flaws.
Your talents in combat are peerless. But what do you truly believe in?
Your Highness—
Impatience.
I said, what you truly believe in. Not me nor Theresa.
You still don't know what you seek, do you?
To protect? That's just empty, self-deceptive sentimentality. When you're unsure of your path, you merely project your motivations onto vague ideals.
I-I've thought about it carefully.
Perhaps.
Then follow Theresa and protect her, but don't blindly adopt her ways. Think for yourself. Manfred found his answer long before you did.
The next time we see each other...
...Nevermind.
Go, Ascalon.
There is nothing else for us to talk about now.
Ascalon falls to one knee, then turns to a mist that closes around Theresis.
The mist trembles and disperses, leaving behind only a stone knife in Theresis's hand.
He recalls the day that he snatched the blade from the child in the Catastrophe.
You were too cold to her, Theresis.
It's a time of parting. You could have shared your mind with her. She has never been adept at expressing emotion.
As her teacher, you're better than me at reaching the teenager side of her.
Do you really have no faith in her choice?
She is welcome back at any time. Manfred remains too complacent in his training. He needs someone to challenge him, a goal to work towards.
I'll tell her.
I wish you would come back, too.
I know.
If we cannot resolve a minor conflict like this, the next one could escalate into a full-fledged civil war.
If it comes to that, all we have dreamed of for a century will be for naught.
If that day comes and if I have to... I'll kill you.
Babel is ready. We will leave with those who are willing to follow.
You've always kept them close.
Yes. Because they need me.
I need you, too. Kazdel needs its King.
...Theresis.
Our people have made their choice. The best course of action, for now at least, is for Babel to back down.
I will continue to call the nourishing rain upon Kazdel, change the city's circumstances, and wait for hatred to subside.
But...
If you become the greatest threat to the searching dreamers in this long and painstaking process, I will destroy you.
So be it.
Mercenaries and Royal Court soldiers line the streets, holding back the restless crowd.

lines.st: It was the King of Sarkaz who brought food and shelter.
It was also the King of Sarkaz who allowed oppression and hatred to fester.

lines.x: The Babel procession stretches as far as the eye can see, past the barricades formed by lines of Royal Court soldiers, past the disdainful crowd.
Theresis walks toward the crowd and stands with them.
He has left Theresa's side for the first time in two centuries.
We all know what we must face.
I hope this will not be the last time we stand together.
...Will that day be far?
Not too far.
Theresa joins the silent procession. Their farewell to the city is soundless. Their path is one of hope.
Then, something occurs, enough to silence the restless onlookers and momentarily halt the procession.
The cursing and crying cease. Every eye turns to a single, poignant scene.
A mercenary steps out of the line to embrace his best friend in the procession.
No one can hear the whispers exchanged between the two friends by the ruins of Babel, but all are willing to wait and give them their moment of farewell.
A farewell between the people and their city, and between two peoples of the same blood.
A young Sarkaz, who has lost everyone he holds dear, walks in the procession without regret.
He remembers walking the same path, leaving the city in search of his parents with his older companion many years ago.
Dad, I believe that there has to be another way than war.
Mom, I know you believed in Babel because you hoped for a different way to live.
Goodbye, Mom, Dad.
Goodbye, home.
For Theresa's eyes only,
I have heard of what happened in Kazdel and share your sense of loss. The city holds special meaning to us all.
But Babel need not drift on the wastelands, for I have located the ship I previously mentioned in Rim Billiton.
The excavation went smoothly, and the ship's basic functions have been restored after two years of work.
It will carry the hopes of Babel into the future. Please wait for me, Theresa.
P.S.: I did find it on the ship.
A legacy that could bring upheaval to this land.
Given its importance, I feel it is crucial to discuss this matter with you in person.
With hope, Kal'tsit
I prepared a gift for you, Doctor.
What do you hear?
This is...
Do you remember? This is the polarization formed when AMa-10 was born. You said you found a rhythm within its waveform. Of course, I added a little personal touch.

I adjusted the direction of the planetary track's array to capture different echoes. The harmony is derived from the lingering neutrinos of extinguishing stars. As for the orchestrator, I used the gravitational folds left behind when the ship sailed through the stargate.
Oh... I knew you'd like it, Doctor.
The universe's singing... Even at this moment, it's still as touching as ever.
But this violates the norms we set ourselves, Priestess. These facilities should've kept operating on the preset path.
The stars are losing their color. This world no longer has norms, doesn't it?
This place will fall silent soon, almost like we've never been here.
Come walk with me, before the silence comes.
I hope... we'll get to see this world's future with our own eyes together, Doctor.

lines.st: 'We will... I will wait for you in that world.'
Four Years Ago...
Summer, 1090

lines.x: Rhodes Island of Babel, Kazdel
Phew! Finally done retrofitting the electrical wiring. Now we're gonna be able to withstand any impact... I think.
How dare Kal'tsit make me work forty-eight hours nonstop... Once all this is over, I'm gonna tell Her Highness on her!
Confirmed within the landship's communications range. Signal's clear. The mini bases you hid around are great, Closure.
Of course! Not even Her Highness has seen anything this handy!
Is their so-called research finished?
The energy surge caused by the last couple rounds almost got Rhodes Island to stall. I'm still reinforcing the landship for the next impact.
You know how much I racked my brains to cover up the landship's movement? Like, what if the Military Commission's spies caught wind—
Relax. I've already eliminated all our targets nearby. Just focus on your preparations.
Scout, report.
The infiltration mission is complete. I'm sending the intel back to Babel.
What's the situation inside the city?
Not much has changed over the past few years. Not many Catastrophes. Very little movement.
Our situation's not very optimistic, actually. If the Military Commission continues to shrink our strategic space—
Let's trust Her Highness and Dr. Kal'tsit.
You're right... They've spent so much time preparing for today's experiment just to turn the tide.
Right, Scout, have you gotten your hands on the equipment I was keeping at my place?
I've already entrusted it to a Messenger to take to Babel.
Awesome. With the secret weapon Kal'tsit and I knocked out together, we'll definitely crack that weird control system on the ship.
I'll rendezvous with the Messenger and confirm the package's risk level before bringing it to the landship.
We have been investigating the agent behind our missing engineer too. The threat against the landship hasn't been fully eliminated yet.
Closure, I brought you even more materials, just as you asked. They're so heavy!
Thanks, Odda! Hm... Looks like now I can make more safety equipment for Her Highness and Kal'tsit.
And I should install an extra line for backup. Hope there won't be any unexpected surprises this time...
Do you need any help, Closure?
Oh. You should hurry and evacuate to a safe zone with everybody else. The next wave of impacts... is probably gonna be in thirty minutes.
Now everything's all ready.
Hm... I'm kinda curious... Just what kind of secret experiments are Kal'tsit and Her Highness running? They even need to mod the energy and defense modules, not to mention those weird tests...
There is no one with a better understanding of Originium.
If you want to solve the suffering caused by Originium...
There is no better candidate for the job.
The news you bring is always so... surprising. Even for the King of Sarkaz...
Are those devices really still working...? Are you confident about the resurrection?
The vast majority of lifeforms on Terra can't resist corrosion that spans such a long stretch of time.
The Terra we see today is no doubt a miracle, and so are you, Theresa.
But in the past, the whole solar system revolving around the sun was insignificant in their eyes.
You seem a little tense... and also a little anxious and nervous.
Is there anything else that worries you?
...Yes.
But... I can't tell you.
It's okay! Sorry, I shouldn't have distracted you. Focus. I can feel it.
'Kal'tsit...' You love this name, don't you?
I... I've forgotten almost everything else about those days.
But I still remember how, in that distant, distant past, somebody gave me this name—
'Kal'tsit.'
That was the last time we ever met.

lines.st: 'Kal'tsit. I do not have much time left.
'Search for traces of life. Search for hope and a future.
'Kal'tsit... Go find your own answer.
'Find yourself.'

lines.x: Then why hesitate?
I know what you mean. It's just...
...I've had a premonition... worry... or perhaps, a conjecture.
At the starting point of this land, at a certain point in time when life had only started budding, something that can never be reversed happened here.
And I don't know what it is.
If it's someone that even Dr. Kal'tsit can put her full faith in, then I'm not one bit worried.
Besides, considering how cautious you are, the safety measures that you prepared well ahead of time have never allowed me to worry.
I'm sorry to say this, Theresa.
This alone, no matter how many contingency measures I prepare, I still can't confidently say anything is conclusive.
Theresa, I can't guarantee that I will always make the most rational decision.
And this time... I want to believe.
Then do what you want to. I'll be here, with you.
Kal'tsit's hand touches the door that has stood silently still for a cryptically long stretch of time.
A new wave of tremors reaches Rhodes Island.
The door loosens.

lines.st: 'Originium will become the beacon, condensed by our civilization...
'If one day, other lifeforms in the universe lose their homeland and seek a way to resolve their plight,
'they will witness...
'...that we once shined,
'we once resisted,
'and this is where we rest.
'We once delivered a gift to those who came after us, before our annihilation—
'—Hope.'

lines.x: (Unknown language) This place...
(Unknown language) Is... it... time already?
Warning: Unknown power activated.
Warning: PRTS access detected...
Warning: PRTS access detect—
PRTS access reset complete. Administrator permissions confirmed.
All communication module channels open. Communications receiver module overclocking procedure completed.
Search: Preserver... No signal.
Search: Caerula Arbor... No signal.
Search: Celestial Fulcrum... No signal.
Search: (Unknown static noise)... No signal.
Search complete. No signal detected on any channel.
Would you like to retry—
(Unknown language) No... answer...
(Unknown language) Just... me...
(Unknown language) How long... has it been?
Searching progress logs...
Latest update: Four million, seven hundred fifty-five thousand, nine hundred and fifty-four days ago.
Abnormal vital signs detected. Loading restoration module.
Restoring—
(Unknown language) Did I... wake up too early... or... too late?
(Unknown language) What stage... has Originium reached?
Searching Originium inspection history data... No response.
Originium inspection module is disconnected.
(Unknown language) 'Terra...'
(Unknown language) Why save... all these... redundant information...
(Unknown language) This... isn't recorded in the database... A new language?
(Unknown language) A new civilization... is born...?
(Unknown language) It's... just... me... What about her...? Priestess? My...
(Unknown language) I can answer your question. I saved those data in PRTS in advance.
(Unknown language) You...?
A hint of unfamiliarity, perplexity, and... vigilance flashed in the eyes of the person stuck in the past.
Kal'tsit senses it from the glare of the person in the sarcophagus.
Likewise, Theresa senses Kal'tsit's nervousness.
(Unknown language) Wait...
(Unknown language) You've changed a fair bit...
The person inside the sarcophagus feels like they have just woken up from a short dream.
The lifeform that bid them farewell prior to sleep is still here by their side.
(Unknown language) ...but you've never left.
(Unknown language) No... From the moment I left this place until now, when I am standing here before you again—
(Unknown language) I have wandered for thousands of years.
(Unknown language) However, I have never forgotten the question you left me—
Kal'tsit waits uneasily. She needs to make sure...
She needs to make sure that her hope has not gone through some kind of horrible change.
(Unknown language) That... was not... a question. That was... what I looked forward to...
(Unknown language) Then... Have you found the meaning of your life yet?

lines.st: 'Kal'tsit...'

lines.x: (Unknown language) Discovering the meaning in the continuation of life... It's a path that I will have to keep walking.
(Unknown language) You still remember...
(Unknown language) I still remember... To me, it was like yesterday when we said goodbye to each other, but it also seemed like an eternity...
(Unknown language) But to you...
(Unknown language) There was never a moment when I forgot all you have done for me.
(Unknown language) Aside from my life, you granted me one other thing that I found most precious—Freedom, Dr. Doctor.
(Unknown language) Please... just Doctor will do, Kal'tsit.
(Unknown language) Let me... get out first...
(Unknown language) You are still very weak. I can help—
(Unknown language) No need...
(Unknown language) Kal'tsit, I still... have many questions.
(Unknown language) What has become of Originium...? I couldn't find any records.
(Unknown language) It still grows on the continent outside.
(Unknown language) So I woke up too early.
(Unknown language) You've suffered, Kal'tsit.
(Unknown language) I can tell from your eyes. You woke me up because you need my help?
(Unknown language) Yes, we need your help.
(Unknown language) I see. That 'devil'?
(Unknown language) I can see from your friend behind you that not only has Originium found an unexpected way to exist...
(Unknown language) She also possesses... yes, Civilight Eterna.
(Unknown language) The course of this civilization is... very complicated.
(Unknown language) What's her name...? Does their civilization have anything like that used to refer to individuals?
Theresa.
The... re... sa...
The awakened one walks up to Theresa and holds out two arms, showing the palms of those hands.
Theresa extends her hands to lightly hold the hands of the one from the past.
The Doctor feels warmth.
Yes, Theresa.
This is the moment two civilizations make contact with one another, yet Theresa can see sadness in the eyes of this Doctor from eras long gone.
And she feels no warmth from the hands she is holding.
Originium... and you... together.
You speak our language?
I... learned... from... records. Not... hard.
Unbelievable! I was thinking about learning your language. Kal'tsit said—
My world... is lost. Languages of the past... should stay in the past.
I... am curious about your kind. You found... and even entered... Civilight Eterna.
I... want to... ask—
Ask you everything about your... civilization.
That's the only way... I can find traces of my world... the world of the past.
...I understand.
Hm, Doctor, Is that how you say it? Please forgive me. Keep holding my hands.
I will show you—
The Doctor cannot help but be attracted to the eyes of this creature that looks like a devil.
Through her eyes, the one who lost a world sees—
Life.
Civilization is born from the instinct of living beings. It's survival of the fittest.
Natural selection. This is how the cycle of life is formed.
(Feeble wailing)
A burdenbeast accidentally fell beneath the ice pushes a small cub up from the water with its head.
The small burdenbeast staggers on the ice and walks toward the hunters who are waiting afar with their bait.
It's coming this way. Get ready.
The hunter grabs the starving burdenbeast cub before the ice cracks underneath them.
Good. At least we managed to save one.
Rebellion. Going against our survival instinct is the catalyst of our civilization's evolution.
And curiosity... curiosity is what we burn our lives away for, the staircase that allow our civilization to proceed forward.
A pioneer chasing the stars is pushing forward with his fallen ally on his back.
*pant*... *pant*... You dipshit... Why can't you keep at it for just one more day?!
The newspapers already said that it was the crashed aircraft of the Wrights, not a shooting star! But you just had to get to the bottom of it!
They were failures who crashed from the sky! What was there to risk your life for?
Those rocks that fell onto the land? What did you want to prove to me?
I, too, once saw within the Black Crown the sun getting engulfed, with darkness shrouding the whole continent.
But even in its darkest hour, there were still those who tried to light up the night.
It's still black! The sun really is dead!
*sigh*... The sun's been dead for too long... We gotta save it! If the sun doesn't live, how are we going to hold our tail thickness contests?
But how?
We climb the tallest mountain, and raise a torch up high to light it back up.
What if our arms get tired?
Hm... then we'll build a statue of me, and my statue will hold the torch up instead.
Good idea! We can build my statue next to yours to hold up another torch, too! Then, if your torch goes out, my torch can light yours!
With fire comes light, and it is light that guides us toward hope.
And my kin see death as a different interpretation of hope.
After your body's destruction, to become one with the city within the Soul Furnace's flames is an honor bestowed only on those who are most respected.
They will grow as Originium crystals and become one with Kazdel.
The newborn will grow among the fluttering dust, and continue their march forward with hardships in tow amid the chorus of the myriad souls.
Progress mixed with ignorance... The cycle continues and repeats over and over.
So... beautiful.
So... nostalgic.
Your kind... Terra seems to have gotten back on the right track... and achieving enlightenment.
How is 'ignorance' defined, and how do we prove the progress of 'enlightenment?'
The way I see it, all of this is part of our civilization.
But the things you showed me... Most of them are... extremely different compared to your race.
Conquerors and the conquered, both have been branded with inerasable marks by this land throughout the evolution of civilization...
For the sake of survival, I believe that all discrepancies will be unified, and all clans will become one.
In the future that I envision, all the many lands will become one.
That's a stirring belief... It's admirable.
I can feel that... you truly love this great land.
Be it discrimination, war, or disaster... The object of your love has long surpassed an individual or a clan.
But if you... surpass the extremes of the civilization you belong to... no one will be able to comprehend your love.
On the contrary... they will loathe you.
Do you understand what you will have to go through... to reach the future you strive for?
If there is no choice but to walk that path, I will gladly do so.
Theresa. A Terran.
No wonder Kal'tsit trusts you. She willingly took the risk to wake me in order to help you.
Right. When every era comes to an end, there are always those who try their hardest to change the tide in the final moments.
But most of the time, their stories don't end well.
Nevertheless, there is no doubt that you have my admiration.
After all, on a certain level, you are very similar to some people that I knew.
I will never get to see them again...
...Perhaps I'll need some more time to get my thoughts in order.
But no matter. I think I should... listen to what you two have to say first.
What do you need me to do?
Doctor, you need rest.
I'm fine.
Kal'tsit. She's given me lots of surprises, but on top of that, she's given me even more challenges.
Sorry... I mean 'confusion'.
I still don't have a very good grasp of the Sarkaz language.
I saw a lot of unexpected things in her. Surely you did too.
I made enemies with her, came to know her, began to understand her wish, and finally admired her responsibility...
That Black Crown shared with Theresa the long history and experience it bears, and she too slowly gained foresight that no other can attain.
I felt... perhaps this would be our best chance. With her, with Babel, and with Terra.
'With Terra.'
You, too, are part of 'this great land' now, Kal'tsit.
I hope you will take this journey with us, too.
Right...
I envy you, and I trust you, Kal'tsit.
But I am just a researcher. Perhaps the help I can offer won't be as significant as you hoped.

Oripathy, Catastrophe... Disasters that we have no methods to resist have been bringing many hardships to these lands... yet Terra's technology is almost entirely built atop the foundation that is Originium.
Fighting over energy, the discrimination against the Infected, and the estrangement between war and races are all further tearing these lands apart...
We tried, but our efforts yielded very little... That's because we know practically nothing about Originium. We know nothing about this great land.
What happened to the sky and the ocean? Where did the ancient ruins come from? How exactly... were we born?
We hope you'll be able to find answers for us.
Surely, Kal'tsit must already have shared some of those with you.
As for Originium, I'm sorry to say that the person I am now can't give you a good answer. At the very least, not an answer to everything.
When I woke up, I tried to access the logs tracking Originium's progress, but strangely, there was nothing.
And this glimpse of Terra that you showed me has made me even more confused.
The development of Originium... has taken a completely different turn from what I expected. I need some time.
I need to explore and see this great land for myself, to understand how exactly Originium has influenced culture, life, and environment.
This could take years, perhaps longer... And maybe, after an enormous amount of energy is expended, you will receive only a most disappointing answer.
I once made a promise to somebody. Thinking about it now, perhaps all I ever left myself or her... was regret.
That's why I won't make a promise lightly, Kal'tsit. Forgive me...
In that case, we will wait.
No matter what your answer will be, Babel will last until the day of your return, Doctor.
...Thank you.
Well... sorry... but I'm a little hungry.
PRTS showed me that the landship has a temporary cafeteria...
That it does, but it might be a little messy there. How about I take you—
Thank you, Theresa, but no.
This place has a special meaning to me, too. Let me walk there, slowly, by myself...
Okay... Be careful, Doctor.
The awakening has given the Doctor a lot of pressure, but it would seem this one is quick to adapt, and much quicker than I expected.
The Doctor wants to learn all there is to learn about Terra.
A quick learner too, it seems.
If the children were like the Doctor, perhaps they would have the chance to master even more languages and travel to so many places across Terra.
Theresa, you should go back and look for the others. There should be plenty of Babel affairs that need the Chairman herself to...
Theresa?
Kal'tsit... there's something bothering me. Maybe I'm overthinking it, but I want to tell you.
The Doctor seems to be very surprised by the look of Terra as it is today.
You once told me that nobody has a better understanding of Originium than the Doctor. The same Doctor who said that the development of Originium... took a completely different turn from the expected.
Then what should Originium have been like? What should these lands have been like?
...What should the Sarkaz have been like?
Is the Doctor ready to depart?
Right. The Doctor applied for a transport, some defensive weapons, and nothing else.
The Doctor stated an intent to avoid combat zones.
Alright. We'll just abide the Doctor's wishes.
Your Highness, I would like to follow this Doctor of ours. Considering the background involved... I am worried.
Ascalon... have you ever left Kazdel?
Left? If we count how I once chased a quarry and left the area when I was small, then I most certainly have.
That doesn't count.
Go, follow and protect the Doctor, but do not interfere.
I will keep a close eye—
No, Ascalon, calm down. I don't want you to keep an eye on anyone.
I want you to go out there and see how the people in other lands live. This is a great opportunity.
How they live?
Outside our homeland, neither war nor suffering is part of people's daily lives. Food, fashion, arts, culture... We could have so much.
Many of us know this fact, but even more of us choose to ignore this fact, because most of the time we don't have the freedom to choose.
Don't give me that look, Ascalon. Listen to me.
At first, you followed me because you opposed Theresis's measures, but what about after this division is over?
What will you do then? What will be the next stop for the countless Sarkaz who share the same idea as you?
That's why you need to take a break from the war, and see the sights beyond our homeland.
See the life that we should have lived.
If that is your wish.
It is!
Have a safe trip.
Dear Kal'tsit,
Glad to receive your letter. Hope you and everyone in Babel are well.
A couple of months ago, I rescued a girl by the name of Amiya from a convoy that had met with misfortune.
The accident killed her parents and afflicted her with Oripathy.
The locals deal with orphaned Oripathy patients by putting them in a car and driving them into the wastes. I had no choice but to take her with me.
We have become fellow travelers by chance.
You can't imagine my panic and helplessness as I took care of her...
Fortunately, a woman who goes by the name of Savage has joined our little band, which has helped take some of the burden off me.
A shadow leaps out of the dunes in the moonlight.
(Approaches cautiously)
(Threatening roar)
Hey! Go away!
Don't come any closer!
(Rubs claws excitedly)
(Moves head in confusion)
You seem to have made them even more excited, Amiya.
Don't worry, Doctor, I can deal with this!
Mum and Dad taught me how to chase the sandbeasts away. All you need to do is imitate the sound of their natural enemies.
Chirp, chirp...
(Pauses in confusion)
Wait, that wasn't right. Was it 'Chip, chip'?
(Tilts head in confusion)
It's still not right. What was it again?
'Aoww'? 'Ku, ku'? 'Tick, tock'?
(Swings tail in confusion)
That's not right either...
It's okay, Amiya. I'll take care of this.
The Doctor makes a ring with thumb and index finger, and whistles, producing a strange sound.
(Stands up startled)
(Looks around uneasily)
(Quickly digs a hole)
(Slowly follows)
Wow, Doctor! That was just like the real thing!
Let me try!
Amiya presses her tiny hand against her lips, but used too much force, and only gets a sound out after going all red and puffy in the face.
The pair of whistles echo across the wastes, sending the sandbeasts scurrying underground.
A child's laughter replaces the whistles.
Then the laughter fades away to silence, and a soft little sigh.
Come here, Amiya.
Why the sullen look? The sandbeast ran off.
I could never remember, even after Mum and Dad taught me so many times... and I couldn't protect you, even though I said I would.
Your hand's still wounded... it hasn't fully healed.
Don't worry, it doesn't hurt. Anyway, don't you remember?
It was you who taught me how to make that sound.
I did?
You see? Like I told you, your brain slows down and you start to forget things, if you don't sleep.
I... I don't want to sleep.
I'm scared that I'll dream of... those things.
But you told me you dream of your parents too.
Um...
We have some time before our ride gets here. Have a sleep.
Hm...
Can I come closer?
When I wake up... the first thing I want to see is you.
Of course. Why don't I tell you a few stories while we wait for Savage?
A long time ago, there was a really smart scientist...
Her home was in danger, and she made a difficult choice to protect it and the people around her...
Doctor, I found a vehicle! We'll be able to go to the next—
(Whisper) Shh, she's sleeping.
Dear Kal'tsit, I was glad to find signs of life wherever I went.

This should be an unfamiliar world, yet there are times when it feels incredibly familiar. I recall the legends that had fascinated me in the past, never failing to capture my imagination despite their age.
The ancient machines that had rumbled in the mines and dust once again roam this land.
Stories can become reality, and the reality we see can become stories.
You can come over, Amiya. No need to keep poking your head out from there.
Are you drawing, Doctor? Am I interrupting?
Of course not.
Take a look. Is it good?
It's the mining truck we just drove together!
I've been drawing lots of our experiences.
Have you driven a mining truck before? Even the old miners can't go as fast as you did.
I've seen many amazing vehicles before. Like Originium slug eggs, but much larger. Once, I drove one of them...
...and went through the bridge between stars.
The bridge between stars? It's always dark at night, so it must've been really dark! Was it dangerous?
No. Guides stand by the bridge, borrowing glowing clouds from old stars and making round mirrors out of them.
Follow the path the mirrors show, and we can...
We can...?
See the spires fading in and out of the clouds, and the crown of flowers worn by the stars, on the other side of the bridge.
When we descend on the crown, you'll find that it belongs to good children like you. It's a really, really big playground.
A playground? I've read about them in the stories... there are lots of children there.
Do you want to play with other children?
No. I... I just want to play with you, Doctor.
You'll make more friends as you grow up.
As I grow up...
What's the most amazing vehicle you've ever driven?
The most amazing...? Well... I spent a very long time with it. Very long indeed.
Was there anyone else with you?
In the beginning, yes. In the end, though, I was alone.
...But it's alright. I was sleeping. I slept for a very long time.
Take me with you next time! That way, I can keep you company and you won't have to sleep all day.
...Hehe.
Why are you laughing, Doctor?
That's a big promise to make. Are you sure?
You'll have your own journey, your vision, your future... are you sure you want to be with me?
But they all say that... there's no future for people with rocks on them.
So... I'll always be with you...
We'll have a future, Amiya.
How's Amiya doing? Still not great?
Hm... still a bit of fever. Maybe Oripathy complications from all the excitement. Hard to find Oripathy meds around these parts.
She acts like it's nothing during the day, but keeps having nightmares. Poor girl...
Every survivor of that accident was infected. No exceptions.
How long does it take for Oripathy to progress in a child her age?
It's different for everyone. Some disintegrate the day after developing acute symptoms. Some suffer for more than a decade of misery.
But the end is always the same.
Oripathy... is there anyone in the world who can cure it?
The world? Oh, you mean Terra, our great land? Man, you sound like one of those old scholars. I didn't know you had a sense of humor.
Wait... you're serious?
I'm afraid not. It's impossible.
I need it to be possible.
People couldn't imagine the nomadic cities a few centuries ago, could they?
I'll find a way, before her symptoms deteriorate. It may be hard, but that doesn't mean it's impossible.
Say it to a Rim Billy, and they'll think you're making fun of them.
You never fail to surprise me, but I just can't help but believe in you.
Is that the charm of being smart?
Come on, Charlotte.
Hehe, I meant you... never mind.
What?
Oh, I meant... hm...
Can't you tell? There's something about you... something that lets everyone know that you're different from us.
Amiya senses it. Children are more sensitive than we know.
Is it that obvious?
She's strong. She doesn't show her sadness in front of you. She wants you to see her joy. She doesn't want to be a burden to you; she's trying to take care of you.
You became the only pillar in her life the day you saved it.
But she knows you were never supposed to cross paths. She's afraid that you'll abandon her too.
Don't worry. I won't leave her.
Now that's the spirit.
I knew you would say that.
Dear Kal'tsit, I thought I would never be more a passing visitor in this place, one history would never remember.
This intentional isolation made me forget reality: I am here, on Terra, this great land.
I don't know what made me feel it.
But the warmth of life coming from a child's palm stings my heart and makes it burn.
This is...
Jerky and fruit. Left here on purpose.
They know I'm here?
Always inscrutable.
Dear Kal'tsit, I'm planning to take Amiya back to Babel.
Savage has done her best to find medicine for Amiya, but my ability to treat Amiya's Oripathy is limited under present conditions.
I know that Amiya isn't special, by any objective standard. I know that Babel faces a crisis no less complicated, but I've decided to bring her back.
I met many people along the way. I've fretted over money at times, benefitted from people's generosity at others. I've seen new things, even some that surprised me for the first time in a long while.
The convoy that we were traveling with broke down. We found a herder to guide us through the wilderness of Rim Billiton on the backs of burdenbeasts, to the rumbling of drills and engines.
(Licks docilely)
Good boy, good boy.
Are we really not going to Yan, Doctor?
We're going somewhere else, to cure your sickness.
But... there's no cure for Oripathy...
There's no such thing as an impossible puzzle, Amiya.
There's a place called Babel, where people I trust are working hard to fight this disease.
Let's work with them.
Will I have to spend a long time there?
Some time, at least. But don't worry, there'll be a lot of people with you, caring for you. I'll be there, too.
Doctor... do we really have to go?
Lots of sick kids went away to get treated, but no one came back...
Oripathy doesn't hurt at all.
I promise I'll be good, Doctor. Right, Savage?
Yes... Amiya has always been a good girl.
Are you sure you don't want to rethink this, Doctor?
Billies deal with Originium every day. We know what Oripathy can do.
Stay here in Rim Billiton, and at least we can give her... a home.
Maybe that's more important than the cold walls of a laboratory.
...A home.
You have a point. But I can't just stand by and watch Originium hurt her... I have an obligation.
Amiya, I promise you, I'll do my best to cure you, no matter what happens.
Doctor...
Savage does have a point, though, and Babel is in a place that's not entirely safe.
You can stay with Savage, if you don't want to leave Rim Billiton.
I'm sorry to force this decision on you at such a young age... but you're a strong girl, and we'll respect your choice.
The Doctor's right, Amiya. No need to knee-jerk an answer. Think carefully about it.
...I trust you, Doctor.
I want to have a future. I want to grow up, read books with you, travel with Savage. I...
It's just that... I'm a little scared...
Heh.
Thank you, Amiya.
The Doctor pats Amiya's head, then picks her up and climbs onto the burdenbeast.
They've hardly sat down when the ground begins to shake, stirring up clouds of dust.
(Growls in alarm)
What's going on?
—Underneath our feet!
Must be a deep digger! It's spooking the burdenbeast! Watch out!
(Panicked cry)
Stay, boy, stay! Whoa!
Whoa!
Hang in there, Doctor!
Doctor!
The burdenbeast rears up on its hind legs, gives a loud cry, then charges forward.
The rest of the pack follows.
They run towards a shallow shoal, splashing water everywhere.
It is all the Doctor can do to grab the fur on the back of the burdenbeast and hang on for dear life.
Amiya's eyes are wide-open in the chaos.
With one hand on the burdenbeast, she stretches out the other towards the Doctor.
Grab my hand, Doctor!
(Angry roar)
(Accelerates)
I have to get them to—whoa!
Hang on! It's speeding up!
I'm—try—ing!
Hang in there, Doctor! I'm coming!
Hur—ry!
I—
Whooooaaaahhhh—!
In the end, I was thrown from the back of the burdenbeast into the water.
My hindquarters and back burned with pain, and I gagged on the water rushing into my nose.
But it also made me feel like I was truly living on this land, after spending almost half a year traveling across it.
I heard the sound of my life surging.
Summer, 1091
Kazdel
...No more Messenger stations from here on.
Villages become sparse, and bandits become frequent...
I'm afraid we have to part ways here, Savage.
Why?
The region ahead is in turmoil. You'll be in danger, if the mercenaries spot you.
You and Amiya will be in danger too, won't you?
I'll be fine! I've come this far, I'll see your journey through to the end.
Charlotte... thank you.
The Cautus's ears quiver at the mention of her real name. She glances at the Doctor.
A few months is neither long nor short.
True, it comprised only a tiny part of her lifetime, but it was also a span of a few hundred days, filled with countless unforgettable experiences.
Under the setting sun, before the sandbeasts, beneath the eaves. Upon this land.
She had enjoyed the journey, even if she never fully understood her quiet companion, and the many strange and curious ideas that companion had.
The Cautus who goes by the name of Savage sighs. What can she do? She is not as smart as the Doctor.
Will you come and visit, Savage?
Of course!
The Doctor said Babel's new base is being worked on by Billy engineers, right? Maybe we'll see each other soon!
Then... I'll be waiting for you, Savage.
Good girl, Amiya. Now, though, I need to figure out how to take you and the Doctor through the wilderness!
You and Amiya stay here and rest for a while, Doctor. I'll see if the transport hub ahead has any good vehicles for hire.
Doctor.
Whoa!
Do we have time to say goodbye?
The longer we stay here, the more dangerous it gets.
The Cautus girl seems rather determined. It's better for both of you to part ways here.
...I suppose you're right.
We won't wait for Savage?
Sometimes, Amiya, we have to do bad things for good reasons.
We don't want Savage to be in danger, do we? But she doesn't want to leave you.
It's rude to leave without saying goodbye, but that way, Savage won't face danger.
Um...
I promise, we'll get to see her again.
Can I leave a note for Savage? She'll be worried if she comes back to find us gone.
Good thinking. Go ahead and write one.
Okay!
Her Highness wants to meet with you, alone.
Are things not going well? How's Kal'tsit?
Now is not the time.
We'll leave once the girl has written her note.
Alright. Can we trouble you with the note?
Certainly. I'll leave it where she is sure to see it.
Autumn, 1091
Rhodes Island of Babel, Kazdel
How's progress, Closure?
The last thing I need is another core system control failure.
Well... Heh, since you had to bring it up, I might have run into a little problem.
Even with the tricks you taught me, the encryption methods used by many of PRTS's programs are almost incomprehensible to a normal brain, and my decoder isn't fully compatible yet.
We may not be able to shut the system down before the Doctor arrives on the ship.
Try again.
If you're not confident about getting it done, then we'll go to the contingency plan... cutting off power to the entire ship.
Leave it to me!
Seems like you're still keeping a lot of secrets, though.
I've never seen anyone make you so nervous.
...That's not important.
What's important is making sure nothing happens to the ship.
Ascalon's report says the Doctor looks like an ordinary person. A pretty decent one, too, who picked up a stray little Cautus and carried her all across the land.
Do we really have to be so on edge?
It's not the Doctor that I fear.
Then what is it?
You don't need to know yet.
Wipe the Doctor's boarding records. This is for the safety of both the Doctor and Babel.
The others don't need to know that the Doctor exists until the Doctor and Theresa have reached a conclusion.
Alright, alright, I'll work at it.
But I wonder why you seem to care a lot about this Doctor at that same time that you're so on guard over the whole thing.
I've got it! The Doctor must have dirt on you!
Don't bother, you won't find it.
Come on, why so serious?
What about the girl?
Same conclusion, no change.
I cross-checked all relevant Rim Billitonian records and had Scout check with informants inside the Military Commission.
Amiya's background comes through clean.
Her parents were killed when the convoy transporting the laser mining module got attacked. The attack itself was purely coincidental.
No one knew they were transporting Sarkaz-related cargo, and the Military Commission wasn't involved.
She must have gotten Infected in the mess. If not for the Doctor, she'd be spending the rest of her life suffering in Rim Billiton.
I'll handle the girl's examination and treatment once she's on board.
You're still concerned about her?
Yes, I am.
She's too young, and she hasn't had proper treatment since contracting Oripathy.
Oh, that kind of concerned.
I don't want the Doctor's kindness to be the reason for delaying her treatment.
Plus, Theresa would call her a gentle child who has the right to choose her future.
Amiya. Amiya.
Mm... let me sleep... where are we?
Doctor... Doctor?
The Doctor's here.
Wake up, sleepyhead. You're almost home.
Home?
In her half-awake state, Amiya feels a warm fingertip brush her cheek.
Haha, that tickles, don't do that, hehe.
Whoa!
Wh-Who are you?
I'm Theresa.
Theresa... the Doctor talked about you...
Oh? What did the Doctor say?
I don't remember... I was having a great carrot cake at the time, though.
Theresa should smell nice... be really warm and sweet...
What about now? Is she as you imagined her?
Yeah... smells good... warm and sweet... doesn't smell like carrots, though...
Not fully awake, I see. Heh.
Will you come home with us, Amiya?
Home... yeah... okay...
Outside Rhodes Island of Babel, Kazdel
The tiny Cautus sleeps soundly in Theresa's arms, leading against her neck.
She pats Amiya on the back and turns toward the Doctor, who is clearly tired.
Thank you, Theresa. I didn't expect you to come pick us up personally.
Looks like you're not quite all-knowing, Doctor.
You have much to learn when it comes to childcare, at least.
Sure enough.
You look different from when you left, Doctor. How was the journey?
I saw a lot and learned a lot. There are plenty of interesting things on this great land.
I also met Amiya... it was a miracle that I was able to save her.
Amiya. She's even cuter than your letters said. Poor girl, but fortunate, in some way.
Her condition is getting worse. I needed to bring her in for treatment.
How has Babel been, in the time that I was away?
Not quite smooth sailing. We have a lot to deal with, but not everything needs to be handled right away.
You must be tired after your journey. Get some rest.
Thank you, Theresa.
Amiya, we're home.
Hmm... Doctor? Did I fall asleep?
Oh, Amiya. You're awake.
Why don't you come down? Theresa will get tired if she has to keep holding you.
Amiya nods and jumps down from Theresa's arms.
Will you hold my hand, Amiya?
Doctor...?
It's alright. Theresa is my friend. You can trust her. You'll be spending a lot of time with her from now on.
Okay...
Is this Babel?
Yes.
It's so big... even bigger than those mining plates. So this is where you used to live.
I spent a long time here... a very long time.
Is this your home?
Yes, Amiya, this is the Doctor's home. Right, Doctor?
...Yes.
It's your home now, too. Will you let us help the Doctor take care of you?
Alright... I'll go wherever the Doctor goes.
They step onto the elevator, and the Babel logo appears before their eyes.
The heavy door opens to welcome them in.
Sensing Amiya's unease, Theresa puts a hand on her shoulder.
What's behind the door?
People waiting to welcome you and the Doctor.
Are you sure about taking me and the girl in, Theresa?
Why shouldn't I be? I'm glad to have more people calling Babel their home.
Many here don't know you, though, so I'm sure you'll be busy for the next few days.
How should I present myself?
Good question. You should talk to Kal'tsit first. She's been waiting for you.
Tell her your concerns, your questions, and the secrets in your heart.
She would be very sad if you didn't.
You could have...
If you really want the King of Sarkaz to reveal the thoughts that you're loath to tell, I will. But I can feel you're still wavering.
Let's go, Amiya, Doctor. We'll go to her.
Amiya. You're Amiya, aren't you?
Yes...
Kal'tsit crouches down and gives the girl a candy.
Thank you for protecting the Doctor throughout your journey, Amiya.
Thank you... Dr. Kal'tsit.
Will you fetch Theresa? We need to do some checks on you.
Will they hurt?
A little. But the Doctor's letters called you a brave girl.
Will the Doctor be with me?
Of course. The Doctor has some things to do first, though.
It's alright, Amiya. Just wait for me here in the ward, and I'll be back very soon.
...Okay.
You'll need to do a full checkup later too, Doctor.
Alright.
...Doctor.
Yes?
Do you plan on leaving again?
No, I think I've traveled enough.
If you've made up your mind, I'll respect your decision.
If nothing else, you're concerned about Amiya's health... even though she was a complete stranger to you.
There is no fundamental difference between these new lives and us.
...I'm glad.
Glad that you chose hope, in the end.
Welcome back, Dr. Doctor.
How is Amiya?
Blood Originium levels are high. Currently available inhibitors are not sufficient to stabilize her condition.
We need to develop new drugs... I need time and resources.
Babel's are yours to use, Doctor. We're happy to share our research on Oripathy with you.
What can I help you and Kal'tsit with in exchange?
This is not a transaction. It's one of the core missions of Babel, to do everything in our power to help everyone suffering from Oripathy.
Since the day that Kal'tsit woke you up, I was convinced that you would help with all of this, just as you helped Amiya.
Thank you.
Now, perhaps, I have a chance to ask you this question, Doctor.
How do you view this land, Terra?
I don't think it's my place to judge.
There's no such thing as superior or inferior civilizations. It took a long and hard journey for Terra to evolve to where it is today.
Add to that the sick joke that Originium has played on life...
You said that Originium did not develop the way you expected.
It wasn't supposed to bring such suffering.
(Mumbles in her dream)
I sense that you are keeping secrets, but there's nothing false about the sorrow coming from deep within your heart, the sorrow that I can't understand.
I want to fix this mistake, but I'm powerless to do so...
At the end of the day, civilizations largely progress in similar ways. It all comes to an inevitable endpoint, however bright its light shines at its peak.
But Kal'tsit said you didn't give up hope, even in your final days.
I had many friends, even in those last days, who faced difficulties far greater than you can imagine, with courage and hope that is every bit your equal.
But they failed and faded away, without exception.
We haven't known each other that long, Theresa. I want to know: Why do you do all this?
What is the end that Babel seeks in compromising with the Military Commission?
There's something I want to show you, Doctor.
We had to ask you about Originium, sooner or later. That's why Kal'tsit woke you up.
Please, come and see the results of my... brief time at work.
We're almost there...
This place is barren, even by the standards of a Catastrophe-heavy region. But Closure has spent time and effort to maintain this dangerous little trail...
A forked trail in the midst of Originium formations. You're not keeping any fewer secrets than I am, Theresa.
A King of Sarkaz has to have a few secrets, right?
Don't tell anyone what you see here.
A long trek through the Originium-filled wasteland leads to a small garden.
In the heart of a Catastrophe, amidst the ruins, on a nameless plain that neither night nor the stars can find.
Tiny white specks mark this little sanctuary, isolated from the rest of the world, and the wild lands around it. Even the winds seem softer here.
It is a forgotten greenhouse that harkens back to a time in the distant past, when the land was still pure and untainted.
Like when man stole fire from up above and lit up a corner of the night sky.
Theresa picks up a white flower.
This place represents the cutting edge of Babel's and my research into Originium.
Flowers...?
No. Originium.
I'm sure you understand it's not Originium Arts that made these flowers bloom.
The information inherent in Originium, the reversal of the Assimilated Universe...
You've already come this far? When did...?
No race understands Originium better than the Sarkaz. Still, what we know is merely the tip of the iceberg compared to the entirety of Originium.
Even with Kal'tsit helping us understand the nature of Originium, the furthest we have gone is transforming a palm-sized piece of active Originium into this...
Garden.
Hope.
This little flower is my small contribution. I need your help, Doctor.
I want to see the day when flowers cover the cliffs and valleys of Kazdel, and all the lands beyond.
It's a future worth taking risks for.
You've seen how Kazdel has been mired in war for its entire history, Doctor.
Right now, at least, I want to use the power of Originium to create a home for Kazdel where people can live in peace, safe from war and invasion.
In the more distant future... if we're able to fully harness it, we'll be able to change the entire face of Terra.
If Catastrophes don't bring rain of Originium, but soft fluffy clouds...
If the Infected no longer suffer the pain of rocks growing out of them...
I don't suppose there's anyone who would have such an absurd dream.
You're different from other Sarkaz, Theresa.
Flowers that cover the entire land...
What a picture of life that would be.
Doctor?
Why... why this flower?
Oh... there's no particular reason. I just happened to come across it outside the valley when I was looking for a good place for my experiments.
A pure coincidence.
Kal'tsit said it had a name. 'Snow-in-summer'.
But it's adapted to changes over the years. Perhaps we could give it a new name.
What do you think, Doctor?

lines.st: A new path is opening up.
A path to self-destruction.

lines.x: Can you hear me, Theresa?
I'm listening. I was giving the Doctor a tour of our...
Urgent news from Kazdel.
It concerns Victoria.

lines.st: Your Highness Theresis, Regent of Kazdel, Commanding General of the Military Commission,
Wandering scholars have brought back tales of a new nomadic city on the rise in the distant desert.
The fires of the ancient furnace continue to burn, and the once-scattered mercenaries have flocked to one banner, to the city walls that grow taller by the day.
Your strength and reputation have caught the attention of the Duke of Cavendish.
You have changed the way we view the Sarkaz tribes. Perhaps now is the time to set prejudices aside, and engage in sincere sharing and cooperation that would be beneficial to us both.
I, the Duke of Cavendish, hereby invite you to visit my demesne, where we may sit and parley.
Our venerable empire has use for you and your brave warriors.

lines.x: Winter 1091
One more time... I have to do this...
No point talking about curing Oripathy, if I can't do something so simple...
Argh—
Still no good... *pant* *pant*
Excuse me, why don't you put that medkit down? Leave this stuff to us.
A-Alright...
Some time in the gym would do you good, once you've recovered.
At present, there is no way for you to match the physical prowess of a Terran through training.
Your talents are better employed outside of manual labor.
I know. It's just that, you know, I have my pride too...
You've looked on edge since that day.
I'm thinking about... Theresa.
Traveling alone to a city under enemy control isn't the most logical course of action for a leader.
The warriors with her may be outstanding, but they're not enough to protect her in the face of an entire army.
The King of Sarkaz enjoys unimaginable status in Sarkaz tradition, and Theresa's own achievements are well-recognized. The Military Commission would not act rashly.
But Victoria's invitation to Kazdel cannot be taken as a sign of goodwill. It means that Terra's powers have once again set their sights on Kazdel.
Theresa needs to go to Kazdel and make it clear that she will not permit Kazdel to become embroiled in another war.
Even if I know how the Military Commission and Babel came into being...
In my understanding, the Sarkaz can hardly afford an internal conflict that could drain them of what little resources they have left. They have enough to deal with as it is.
They have a complicated history, and that brings different attitudes towards outsiders.
The Sarkaz people have a right to choose the path they believe is correct: Theresis's, or Theresa's.
Looks like we've got a lot to deal with besides Oripathy.
There's a big difference between knowing and understanding, I guess. Looks like there's much I still need to learn about the history of Terra.
This time, you'll be the one to guide me, Kal'tsit.
...Of course.
All we can do right now is make sure that the Oripathy patients at Rhodes Island have access to proper treatment—
What's going on—
An attack? Now?
Word is Her Highness has set out.
Listen up! We exalt only Her Highness. Destroy Babel, and she will return to the embrace of Kazdel.
If we're branded traitors, so be it. But the Regent must not be hindered at this crucial juncture. Kazdel will take back all that we have lost.
May the Sarkaz be united as Her Highness wills.
Scar Market, Kazdel
Her Highness is coming...
Her Highness? The one who's still on the run?
His or Her, it's not your place to talk shit.
Hah. Say, did you take that job?
Wouldn't pass up an opportunity like this if it's the last thing I did. Londinium, that den of scum.
They say the columns in the Feline palace are covered in gold, and the nobles walk on floors paved with glowing stones.
*Sarkaz expletive*, capital of a great empire! A commission from a grand duke! There's gonna be so much money!
It's been a while, Ascalon.
It's been so long since you last visited. Why not sit down and catch up for a bit?
You're sending mercenaries to Londinium.
Now, now, I must object to that accusation.
People just follow the money, and it leads to what the Regent has provided in Londinium.
You're dragging Kazdel into another war.
'Dragging Kazdel into another war.' Have you been fooling around with those dreamers at Babel for so long that you've forgotten what it means to be a Sarkaz?
Since when have the Sarkaz been apart from war?!
The giant's hand lunges forward. Ascalon is surprised to find that she cannot avoid his fist.
...Grr.
Seeing the future is hard. Seeing through what you will do next, less so.
Did you think you were the child of destiny, so special that you could bridge the rift between the siblings?
Think back to the day when you saw them for the first time...
When you skinned that young mangler and followed the trail of blood through the wilderness, until you walked into a storm and met the King of Sarkaz...
How did you...?
'How did I know?' Are you surprised, Ascalon?
I saw the prophecy and led the tribe on the wastes into the storm that day with game. You happened to be the one who lived.
No one truly understands prophecy. No one can interfere with the future. But I wanted to catch the tail of fate... I wanted to make prophecy.
Theresis didn't kill you. Instead, he kept you and raised you. Maybe he wanted to tempt fate.
But you never changed, in all these years. You're still that poor, lost child on the wastes.
Have patience, Ascalon.
You're not a threat to me yet. And I have higher hopes for you than this.
Master said—
'Your blade points at all the enemies of the Sarkaz.'
Theresis raised you to be a deathmonger, yet you understand less about him than a warmonger like me.
Did you think Theresis would be content to be some puppet for those dukes? Spare your breath, if you don't know the full extent of his plan.
I'm curious, how would you stand before Theresis, if he was sitting in the room behind me right now?
You're simply repeating the words of others, on behalf of the one behind you.
You seem to have put a lot of thought into this war.
How many hostiles?
Exact numbers unclear. We've detected hostiles both in front of and behind us. A lot of them...
Whether it was Theresis's intention or not, the other generals of the Military Commission seem intent to crush Babel while Theresa is absent.
That way, the Sarkaz won't have a choice.
How many combat personnel do we have?
The best soldiers went into negotiations with Her Highness. A good part of the remaining ones have been assigned to the supply convoys. We only have less than one third of our normal force.
At the very least, we need to break through the roadblock ahead...
...Kal'tsit.
I'll take command.
Doctor?
If, as you said, this attack was launched by only a few members of the Military Commission, then they're undoubtedly planning to get this done in one go.
We can't let them set the pace.
Find out where their lines are thin. Disrupt their rhythm, and we'll be safe.
Have you fought a war before, Dr. Doctor?
Not the flesh and blood kind... but I've been trained.
I'm able to reconstruct a battlefield as a computable data model.
Take the front line, Kal'tsit. I know you'll be able to protect us.
Of course.
Welcome to the Scar Market, Your Highness.
Things must be busy here lately.
No need going against the will of the many. Am I right, Your Highness?
The voices of the few are worth respecting too.
Many know nothing of what's happening in the lands beyond. Their lives will be turned upside-down. They'll be without a home once again.
I'm just a humble merchant. My business only stretches so far.
Mercenaries seek to sell their lives. I seek to make money. That's why I always respect the positions of my big clients.
Then I'll talk to your biggest client. The one in the room behind you.
Your Highness should go no further.
Since when have you started meddling in the affairs of Kazdel, Scareye?
All the more reason I can't let you go further.
Well...
Please, don't make things difficult for Scareye, Your Highness.
You understand that there's only one way this can go, if you and the Regent come face-to-face here.
...Confessarius.
I beg Your Highness's pardon.
Looks like you've made a decision.
Is this Theresis's position?
It is.
He has decided to abandon our city?
Not abandon. Take a step forward, to go on a long expedition away from home, that's all.
You have made us stagnant. We want to progress.
The Confessarius is right.
I still remember when you and General Theresis came here for the first time. Even fate itself stepped aside for the two of you, when you stood together.
But look at the people here now, when Kazdel is divided.
You can still command any Sarkaz to your will as their King. You could kill all of us who stand here right now. But why not ask them if they're still willing to follow your dream?
The bustling Scar Market has lost its usual commotion. Scattered mercenaries stow their weapons and stand with heads bowed in reverence.
In their eyes is silence, but the King of Sarkaz hears the voices of their hearts clearly.
Uncertainty, excitement, desire, anger.
Most of you have made up your minds.
Then there is no point for me to try to stop you.
One of the Sarkaz suddenly kneels.
He has not changed his mind. He has not turned away from the temptation of Theresis's plan.
He simply feels a sense of guilt towards Theresa, one of the Six Heroes, their King who rebuilt Kazdel.
Kneel if you feel you should. Neither General Theresis nor Lord Scareye will fault you.
Ah... this ancient gesture signifies more than just fealty. I am more familiar with its other meaning...
Penitence.
...Qui'sartuštaj.
Have you found the meaning of your name?
Not yet, Your Highness.
Then forgive their sins as you must forgive yours.
Theresis and I will carry all your sins.
As you bid.
I bow to you, King of Sarkaz.
In millennia of Sarkaz history, it has always been an honor for warriors to go to war with their Kings.
They have tasted struggle and glory, picked themselves up from the bloodied soil...
For the Kings of Sarkaz always stood in front of them, never abandoning their people.
Where the Kings point, that is where home is, where cities will emerge from the earth.
Now, though, they have abandoned their King.
I promised a home of peace and safety, where Sarkaz will be forever free from war.
Before I set foot here, I had a sliver of hope that perhaps someone would follow me.
But I saw fire in your eyes.
I saw the city that none of you had seen with your own eyes burn.
The ancient city comes in many shapes and forms in your eyes.
A city of desire, a city of hatred, a city of wealth, a city of glory...
You all believe you will plunder what you have longed for in that unfamiliar land.
That's why I changed my mind. I decided to see Kazdel's bravest warriors off.
Kazdel will embark on a path of conquest for the first time in many years.
I honor you, even though I never wanted you to take this step.
You answered Theresis's call and dove into yet another campaign of hatred.
Death awaits you far away from home.
Yet you still choose to leave our home and go to Londinium.
The sorrowful King of Sarkaz walks among her people, who bow their heads to avoid her gaze.
Yet their determination does not waver, even if it means defying the King.
'We're going.' 'We're going.'
Voices rise and fall, as though the warriors are singing an elegy for themselves as they go to death.
We exalt Your Highness, but we cannot follow you anymore.
The Regent promises that Londinium will be a new start.
There lies the Sarkaz future.
There lies our future!
Take care of yourselves, my warriors.
Even if you will never return to Kazdel.
The white figure passes through the parted crowd. The King's people are unmoved by her words.
They no longer follow the King they once exalted.
The other possibility for her people, the home full of flowers that she strived to build, did not come in time for the Sarkaz.
General...
You knew this day would come.
What do you think?
Hesitate, and all we've worked for will be for naught.
Her Highness did not hesitate. Neither should we.
Make your preparations, then, Manfred.
Do not forget the feelings that burn within you, here and now.
No matter what they are.
Secret Entrance of Scar Market, Kazdel
No need to be down, Ascalon.
More will join us when Babel grows strong enough to provide shelter for more people.
We're being followed.
Your Highness—!
Wait, Ascalon. She's not hostile.
Whoa, that was a hard hit. Is that a sleeve-blade?
Anyway, Your Highness, I would like to leave with you, if that's okay with you.
Thank you. What's your name?
Julie.
A nice name. One that's not often used by mercenaries. Did you make it up yourself?
No... I don't have a name. Someone else gave it to me. She said having a name gives one a sense of belonging.
She's right.
Why not follow the rest of them to Londinium?
I've had enough of fighting. She says Your Highness will bring about a life where we don't have to fight anymore. That's why I came to check it out for her.
I wanted to challenge you before deciding whether to go with you, but I didn't even have the guts to raise my sword when you stood before me.
You're a strong warrior, that's for sure.
I wouldn't say that about myself when I'm standing in front of you.
At least I can be sure that she wasn't playing me for a fool when she said you were once a great hero of Kazdel.
A reason so simple it's hard to believe.
The red-haired mercenary scratches her head.
Well... I guess I liked the way you talked just now. Not sure I understood most of it, though.
Where's your friend? She can come too.
She's already here. Her sword and her clothes... may be a little loose-fitting, though.
She was a strong warrior. Felt a little like this one over here.
I'm a little something of a tailor. I could hem it for you.
There's no need. I'm used to it.
Let's go, then, Julie.
One moment, please, Your Highness.
There are other mercenaries here who want to go with you.
Theresa looks toward the dark exit of the Scar Market, and sees a swaying speck of flame.
One speck becomes two, two become three, and many more emerge from the darkness. The first torch-carrying mercenary steps into the sunlight and sees the white figure standing on top of the hill.
